EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across NY and northern New England on January 23rd with sub-zero temperatures (5 to 25 below zero) and brisk winds to create dangerous wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero across the region for the overnight of January 23rd into the morning hours of January 24th. Very strong arctic high pressure across the region during the night of January 24th allowed for more sub-zero temperatures (15 to 25 below zero) across the region but winds were calm or light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Actual air temperatures of 10 to 20 below zero combined with winds to create wind chill values of 20 to 40 below zero during the night of January 23rd into the morning hours of January 24th. Morning low temperatures on January 25th were 10 to 20 below zero with light winds creating colder wind chill values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated area of low pressure from the southern Mississippi River Valley to the Carolina coast with strong arctic high pressure north accounted for relatively rare accumulations of snow and ice from New Mexico to the Tennessee river valley on January 24th and 25th. The area of low pressure off the Carolina coast strengthened modestly and moved north along the east coast during January 25th and eventually out to sea southeast of Cape C but north of 40N/70W benchmark.||Snow overspread the region during the afternoon of January 25th with the heaviest during the evening into early morning hours of January 26th with a temporary lull until mid to late morning of January 26th when additional accumulations occurred, especially across northern NY and northern VT.||Snowfall totals were 8 to 10 inches along the international border with Canada with 10 to 18 inches elsewhere across the region. The snow character was light and fluffy (low water content) so there were no weighted tree limbs causing power outages, but there were typical numerous vehicle accidents across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were 8 to 15 inches, including; 15 inches in Keeseville, 14 inches in Saranac and 12 inches in Plattsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front producing warm advection in the mid levels resulted in an accumulating snow across portions of interior Southeastern New York. The first of 2 accumulating snowfalls within 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An advisory level snow fell across the county. Trained spotters reported 5.3 inches in Monroe, 5.1 inches in Chester, 4.7 inches at 1 S Pine Bush, 4.3 inches at Fort Montgomery, 4.0 inches at 2 SSW Middletown, and 3.8 inches at 3 WNW Warwick. The public reported 4.0 inches 2 SSW Stewart Airport, and 4.8 inches at 2 E Highland Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. This produced a heavy snowfall across all of Southeastern New York, with sleet, some of it heavy across a large portion of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 17.6 inches in New City, 13.5 inches in Thiells, 12.5 inches in Chestnut Ridge, 12.5 inches at 1 NNW Valley Cottage, 12.5 inches in Ramapo, and 12.2 inches in Nyack. The public reported 14.2 inches at 1 E Congers, and Cocorahs reported 14.6 inches in Stony Point. Average snowfall amounts across the zone were over a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread synoptic snow caused a moderate snowfall across Western and North Central New York on Sunday January 25th through the most of the day on Monday January 26th. Snowfall was the result of a couple of passing surface lows over the region, including a stronger coastal low. A weak replenishing arctic front moved across NY and northern New England on January 29th bringing colder air on northwest winds. Minimum air temperatures dropped to 5 to 20 below zero with wind chill temperatures of  15 to 35 below zero across northern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Actual air temperatures around 5 below zero combined with winds to create wind chill values of 20 to 30 below zero during the overnight of January 29th into the midday hours of January 30th.

A retired NYPD Officer collapsed and died at his church in Floral Park while preparing to clear snow for the congregation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 9.0 inches in Massapequa, 11.0 inches at 1 SSW Oceanside, and 10.2 inches at 1 WSW Elmont. Cocorahs reported 11.3 inches at 0.5 SSE Malverne and 10.5 inches at 1 ESE Malverne. The public reported 12.1 inches in North Merrick, and 10.5 inches at 1 NNW Valley Stream. In Massapequa a man died of a heart attack while operating a snow blower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours, ushering in much colder air to the region. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.

A series of passing frontal boundaries, surface lows and troughs during the week of January 18th resulted in yet another snowy period for Western and North Central New York. Most of the snow that fell was the result of lake effect, but periods of synoptic snow along with gusty winds at times resulted in primarily Winter Storm Warnings to be issued for this event. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for two zones for later in this event, where snow was entirely lake effect. Snow squalls also occurred at times with the passage of frontal boundaries and troughs. ||Snowfall amounts of one to three and a half feet were common in the hardest hit areas, while widespread amounts of four to ten inches were common in most other areas across Western and North Central New York. Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.

Low pressure tracked east of the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Northeast coast on Sunday the 25th. Snow spread from south to north Sunday morning, becoming heaviest from mid-morning through the early evening. The snowfall gradually tapered off early Monday morning, though pockets of freezing rain and drizzle lingered across central New York. Light snow showers and flurries continued into Monday afternoon, accompanied by strong, gusty winds that caused localized blowing snow and reduced visibility. By Monday night, lake-effect snow showers developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 11 inches.

By Monday night, lake-effect snow showers developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east of the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Northeast coast on Sunday the 25th. Snow spread from south to north Sunday morning, becoming heaviest from mid-morning through the early evening. The snowfall gradually tapered off early Monday morning, though pockets of freezing rain and drizzle lingered across central New York. Light snow showers and flurries continued into Monday afternoon, accompanied by strong, gusty winds that caused localized blowing snow and reduced visibility. By Monday night, lake-effect snow showers developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 9th, a surface low tracked northeast across the Central Great Lakes, into Ontario and then into Quebec. The occluded front associated with the system tracked across the Western and North Central New York areas on the afternoon of the 9th. Winds associated with the system started as strong downslope winds out of the southeast to south for the higher terrain near Lake Erie, but then shifted to the southwest where more winds were felt across a larger portion of Western New York. As the occluded front crossed the region during the afternoon, thunderstorms developed along the front as a line of showers came onshore from Lake Erie. While instability wasn't very favorable, there was a good amount of shear to support some severe thunderstorm potential. Severe thunderstorms were mainly confined to the Western Southern Tier up to the Western and Northern Finger Lakes. Between the strong to damaging winds from the system as a whole and the strong to severe winds from the thunderstorms, there was several reports received for wind related issues for the day.||Some of the notable winds associated with the system include: a 68 MPH at both the Niagara Falls Airport and at the New York State Mesonet in Batavia. A 64 MPH gust at Rochester Airport, 60 MPH gust at the Buffalo Airport and 59 MPH gust at the Dunkirk Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees downed in Perrysburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours, ushering in much colder air to the region. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.

A series of passing frontal boundaries, surface lows and troughs during the week of January 18th resulted in yet another snowy period for Western and North Central New York. Most of the snow that fell was the result of lake effect, but periods of synoptic snow along with gusty winds at times resulted in primarily Winter Storm Warnings to be issued for this event. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for two zones for later in this event, where snow was entirely lake effect. Snow squalls also occurred at times with the passage of frontal boundaries and troughs. ||Snowfall amounts of one to three and a half feet were common in the hardest hit areas, while widespread amounts of four to ten inches were common in most other areas across Western and North Central New York. Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.

Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.

Passing frontal boundaries and troughs crossing the region with cold air in place setup a lake response off of both Lake Erie and Ontario. With Lake Erie mostly ice covered, the lake effect snow was limited and combined with the movement of what lake effect did setup, snow amounts were limited. However, off of Lake Ontario, a more organized lake band setup down wind of the lake, with Jefferson and Oswego counties receiving snowfall amounts of up to 18 inches. ||As colder and drier air moved into the region behind a passing cold front, lake effect snow came to an end on the 29th and temperatures continued to cool into the night of the 29th and morning of the 30th. Overnight lows dropped into the -30s Fahrenheit for areas east of Lake Ontario, with wind chill values down to around -40 Fahrenheit. ||Notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 18.0 inches in Oswego, 17.2 inches in Adams, 12.9 inches in Watertown and 8.2 inches in Copenhagen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed over the upper Ohio Valley along a passing cold front on the afternoon of January 14th. Notable snowfall amounts from around the Buffalo forecast area include: 21.6 inches in Eden, 17.0 inches in North Boston, 16.0 inches in West Falls, 16.0 inches in Newfane, 13.3 inches in North Tonawanda, 12.1 inches in Lockport, 9.5 inches in Perrysburg, 9.4 inches in Mexico, 9.0 inches in Hamlin and 9.0 inches in Cassadaga.

A series of passing frontal boundaries, surface lows and troughs during the week of January 18th resulted in yet another snowy period for Western and North Central New York. Most of the snow that fell was the result of lake effect, but periods of synoptic snow along with gusty winds at times resulted in primarily Winter Storm Warnings to be issued for this event. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for two zones for later in this event, where snow was entirely lake effect. Snow squalls also occurred at times with the passage of frontal boundaries and troughs. ||Snowfall amounts of one to three and a half feet were common in the hardest hit areas, while widespread amounts of four to ten inches were common in most other areas across Western and North Central New York. Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 9th, a surface low tracked northeast across the Central Great Lakes, into Ontario and then into Quebec. The occluded front associated with the system tracked across the Western and North Central New York areas on the afternoon of the 9th. Winds associated with the system started as strong downslope winds out of the southeast to south for the higher terrain near Lake Erie, but then shifted to the southwest where more winds were felt across a larger portion of Western New York. As the occluded front crossed the region during the afternoon, thunderstorms developed along the front as a line of showers came onshore from Lake Erie. While instability wasn't very favorable, there was a good amount of shear to support some severe thunderstorm potential. Severe thunderstorms were mainly confined to the Western Southern Tier up to the Western and Northern Finger Lakes. Between the strong to damaging winds from the system as a whole and the strong to severe winds from the thunderstorms, there was several reports received for wind related issues for the day.||Some of the notable winds associated with the system include: a 68 MPH at both the Niagara Falls Airport and at the New York State Mesonet in Batavia. A 64 MPH gust at Rochester Airport, 60 MPH gust at the Buffalo Airport and 59 MPH gust at the Dunkirk Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines downed in Fillmore.

A potent cold front ushered a bitterly cold airmass into eastern New York on January 23-24, 2026. The combination of bitterly cold air temperatures (minimum temperatures ranging from -34 to 5 degrees Fahrenheit) and gusty winds (ranging from 25 to nearly 50 mph) made for dangerously cold wind chills or feels-like temperatures. Minimum feels-like temperatures ultimately ranged from about -35 to -7 degrees Fahrenheit across the region with the coldest of these confined to portions of the Southern Adirondacks, Lake George-Saratoga Region, and Mohawk Valley.

West to northwest flow off Lake Ontario supported the development of lake-effect snow showers across north-central New York from Friday the 2nd through Saturday the 3rd. The primary snow band initially set up over northern Oneida County Friday morning before shifting south into Onondaga County later in the day. Snowfall rates reached as high as 2 inches per hour for an extended period. The lake-effect snow band gradually weakened and diminished late Saturday night as the wind direction shifted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 7 and 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a widespread light to moderate accumulation of snow to eastern New York on January 17, 2026. The highest snowfall totals occurred across portions of the eastern Catskills, mid-Hudson Valley, Taconics, Saratoga region and into the southern foothills of the Adirondacks with amounts of 3 to 6 inches common. This resulted in slippery travel conditions on area roadways.||Key impacts: travel delays.

The occluded front associated with the system tracked across the Western and North Central New York areas on the afternoon of the 9th. Winds associated with the system started as strong downslope winds out of the southeast to south for the higher terrain near Lake Erie, but then shifted to the southwest where more winds were felt across a larger portion of Western New York. As the occluded front crossed the region during the afternoon, thunderstorms developed along the front as a line of showers came onshore from Lake Erie. While instability wasn't very favorable, there was a good amount of shear to support some severe thunderstorm potential. Severe thunderstorms were mainly confined to the Western Southern Tier up to the Western and Northern Finger Lakes. Between the strong to damaging winds from the system as a whole and the strong to severe winds from the thunderstorms, there was several reports received for wind related issues for the day.||Some of the notable winds associated with the system include: a 68 MPH at both the Niagara Falls Airport and at the New York State Mesonet in Batavia. A 64 MPH gust at Rochester Airport, 60 MPH gust at the Buffalo Airport and 59 MPH gust at the Dunkirk Airport.

An area of low pressure developed over the upper Ohio Valley along a passing cold front on the afternoon of January 14th. As temperatures cooled across the Western and north Central New York areas, rain mixed with and then changed to snow. Snow continued across much of the Buffalo forecast area through the day on Thursday January 15th as the surface low tracked from Northwestern Pennsylvania to Central New York. Cold air filtering into the region, along with mid-level troughs over the region helped increase lake enhancement off of both Lake Erie and Ontario. The snow finally tapered off later in the day on January 15th as drier air and a ridge built into the region. While snowfall amounts weren't overly impressive by Buffalo standards, the widespread nature of the snowfall did cause more disruptions to daily activities than snow from a lake effect event with similar amounts. ||While most snowfall ranged from six to ten inches across most of the Buffalo forecast area, there were some larger amounts closer to the foot and a half to two foot mark where lake enhancement really increased snow rates. Notable snowfall amounts from around the Buffalo forecast area include: 21.6 inches in Eden, 17.0 inches in North Boston, 16.0 inches in West Falls, 16.0 inches in Newfane, 13.3 inches in North Tonawanda, 12.1 inches in Lockport, 9.5 inches in Perrysburg, 9.4 inches in Mexico, 9.0 inches in Hamlin and 9.0 inches in Cassadaga.

A surface low moved into the region from the southwest on Thursday the 15th, with precipitation beginning as rain. As the center of the low passed overhead, colder air filtered in behind it, causing the rain to transition to snow. In its wake, northwesterly flow set up and produced lake-effect snow showers, bringing additional accumulation to North Central NY through Friday morning the 16th. These lake-effect snow showers gradually tapered off by midday Friday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 2 and 6 inches.

A series of passing frontal boundaries, surface lows and troughs during the week of January 18th resulted in yet another snowy period for Western and North Central New York. These passing cold and warm fronts also provided a variability to temperatures during this period as well. Toward the end of the week, behind a passing Arctic front, temperatures dropped to some of the coldest values of the season with daytime highs only in the single digits to low teens for most areas. Overnight windchill values dropped to -30 degrees Fahrenheit or lower at times for Jefferson and Lewis counties on the night of January 23rd. ||Notable windchill values for the end of the week include: -49 degrees Fahrenheit at Copenhagen, -47 degrees Fahrenheit at the Watertown Airport, -46 degrees Fahrenheit at Belleville, and -39 degrees Fahrenheit at Harrisburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous observations and reports from around Jefferson County of wind chill values between -30 and -47 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east of the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Northeast coast on Sunday the 25th. Snow spread from south to north Sunday morning, becoming heaviest from mid-morning through the early evening. The snowfall gradually tapered off early Monday morning, though pockets of freezing rain and drizzle lingered across central New York. Light snow showers and flurries continued into Monday afternoon, accompanied by strong, gusty winds that caused localized blowing snow and reduced visibility. By Monday night, lake-effect snow showers developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 10 and 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of snow showers and localized snow squalls pushed across eastern New York ahead of a secondary cold front during the afternoon hours on January 11, 2026 leading to some brief visibility reductions to one quarter of a mile or less and wind gusts up to 30 mph. Behind the front, strong winds developed across the region with peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph in some locations.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. The New York State Mesonet near Sprakers webcam shows visibilities reducing to around one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 30 mph.

A series of passing frontal boundaries, surface lows and troughs during the week of January 18th resulted in yet another snowy period for Western and North Central New York. Most of the snow that fell was the result of lake effect, but periods of synoptic snow along with gusty winds at times resulted in primarily Winter Storm Warnings to be issued for this event. Lake Effect Snow Warnings were issued for two zones for later in this event, where snow was entirely lake effect. Snow squalls also occurred at times with the passage of frontal boundaries and troughs. ||Snowfall amounts of one to three and a half feet were common in the hardest hit areas, while widespread amounts of four to ten inches were common in most other areas across Western and North Central New York. Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.

In the wake of the clipper snow, scattered snow showers and localized snow squalls pushed across eastern New York during the late morning and afternoon hours on January 22, 2026 along and just behind a cold front. One such squall brought very low visibilities to southern Herkimer County. Thereafter, lake effect snow developed across the southern Adirondacks during the afternoon hours on January 22 and continued into the afternoon hours on January 23 before diminishing. Localized snowfall amounts greater than 7 inches occurred within the most persistent lake effect bands mainly north of Route 28.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile were observed on traffic cameras at the Iroquois Travel Plaza on the New York State Thruway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours, ushering in much colder air to the region. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Snow squalls also occurred at times with the passage of frontal boundaries and troughs. ||Snowfall amounts of one to three and a half feet were common in the hardest hit areas, while widespread amounts of four to ten inches were common in most other areas across Western and North Central New York. Notable snowfall amounts include: 42.0 inches in Lacona, 36.0 inches in Lorraine and Mannsville, 29.2 inches in Eden, 26.0 inches in West Falls, 24.0 inches in Adams and Barnes Corners, 22.0 inches in Colden and Angola on the Lake, 19.5 inches in Elma Center and 19.1 inches in Copenhagen.

Low pressure tracked east of the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Northeast coast on Sunday the 25th. Snow spread from south to north Sunday morning, becoming heaviest from mid-morning through the early evening. The snowfall gradually tapered off early Monday morning, though pockets of freezing rain and drizzle lingered across central New York. Light snow showers and flurries continued into Monday afternoon, accompanied by strong, gusty winds that caused localized blowing snow and reduced visibility. By Monday night, lake-effect snow showers developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the clipper snow, scattered snow showers and localized snow squalls pushed across eastern New York during the late morning and afternoon hours on January 22, 2026 along and just behind a cold front. One such squall brought very low visibilities to southern Herkimer County. Thereafter, lake effect snow developed across the southern Adirondacks during the afternoon hours on January 22 and continued into the afternoon hours on January 23 before diminishing. Localized snowfall amounts greater than 7 inches occurred within the most persistent lake effect bands mainly north of Route 28.||Key impacts: travel delays.

In the wake of the clipper snow, scattered snow showers and localized snow squalls pushed across eastern New York during the late morning and afternoon hours on January 22, 2026 along and just behind a cold front. One such squall brought very low visibilities to southern Herkimer County. Thereafter, lake effect snow developed across the southern Adirondacks during the afternoon hours on January 22 and continued into the afternoon hours on January 23 before diminishing. Localized snowfall amounts greater than 7 inches occurred within the most persistent lake effect bands mainly north of Route 28.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A strong area of low pressure tracked across the central Great Lakes, into southern Ontario and then into southwest Quebec on December 29th. Its strong occluded front crossed the area during the morning into the early afternoon hours, ushering in much colder air to the region. ||The colder air and repeated passages of troughs and cold fronts for the remainder of the New Years week developed into a prolonged lake effect snow event, bringing at least light snow to the entire area. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow at times, combined with breezy conditions, prompted the issuance of Winter Storm Warnings for many counties in the Buffalo County Warning Area. Lake effect bands shifted north to south and south to north with passing troughs and cold fronts during the week, leaving a large footprint of significant snowfall. Snow and breezy conditions impacted New Years Eve celebrations across the areas as well. The hardest hit areas for snowfall were mainly east to southeast of both Lake Erie & Ontario. Some snow amounts from the entire event were as high as 4 to 5 feet off of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.||Some of the notable snowfall amounts from this event include: 59.0 inches in Lacona, 56.5 inches in Richland, 51.5 inches in Colden, 44.0 inches in Glenwood, 34.5 inches in Cattaraugus, 33.6 inches in Silver Creek, 31.0 inches in Highmarket, 29.2 inches in Arcade, 25.5 inches in Batavia and 20.5 inches in Copenhagen.

During the morning of January 11th, a trough over the region helped to initiate a lake effect snow band that briefly develop northeast of Lake Erie. As the troughs axis crossed the Western New York area it pushed the lake effect snow band due east across much of the area south of Lake Ontario. The combination of the lake effect snow band and the increased forcing from the trough axis caused periods of snow squall conditions across portions of Western New York, most notably in the Rochester Metro area and near Wellsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent polar airmass created a prolonged period of frigid conditions across eastern New York for the final days of January into the very beginning of February. Bitter cold air temperatures combined with periods of gusty winds and fresh, deep snowpack made for several days worth of dangerously cold conditions. ||The cold outbreak began early on January 29th where the most dangerously cold minimum wind chill values, or feels-like temperatures, were noted across the higher terrain areas of the Southern Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Mohawk Valley, and Taconics. However, by the following night, widespread dangerously cold temperatures overtook the region with minimum air temperatures ranging from -22 to 6 degrees Fahrenheit and minimum feels-like temperatures spanning -36 to -2 degrees Fahrenheit. A brief respite came on January 30th when extensive cloud cover maintained sufficient insulation to limit temperature falls, but by the next night, February 1, minimum feels-like temperatures once again fell to -34 to -5 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -12 degrees Fahrenheit recorded with a minimum wind chill of -29 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front ushered a bitterly cold airmass into eastern New York on January 23-24, 2026. The combination of bitterly cold air temperatures (minimum temperatures ranging from -34 to 5 degrees Fahrenheit) and gusty winds (ranging from 25 to nearly 50 mph) made for dangerously cold wind chills or feels-like temperatures. Minimum feels-like temperatures ultimately ranged from about -35 to -7 degrees Fahrenheit across the region with the coldest of these confined to portions of the Southern Adirondacks, Lake George-Saratoga Region, and Mohawk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -11 degrees Fahrenheit reported along with a minimum wind chill of -22 degrees Fahrenheit.

However, by the following night, widespread dangerously cold temperatures overtook the region with minimum air temperatures ranging from -22 to 6 degrees Fahrenheit and minimum feels-like temperatures spanning -36 to -2 degrees Fahrenheit. A brief respite came on January 30th when extensive cloud cover maintained sufficient insulation to limit temperature falls, but by the next night, February 1, minimum feels-like temperatures once again fell to -34 to -5 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill of -11 degrees Fahrenheit recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front ushered a bitterly cold airmass into eastern New York on January 23-24, 2026. The combination of bitterly cold air temperatures (minimum temperatures ranging from -34 to 5 degrees Fahrenheit) and gusty winds (ranging from 25 to nearly 50 mph) made for dangerously cold wind chills or feels-like temperatures. Minimum feels-like temperatures ultimately ranged from about -35 to -7 degrees Fahrenheit across the region with the coldest of these confined to portions of the Southern Adirondacks, Lake George-Saratoga Region, and Mohawk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -11 degrees Fahrenheit reported along with a minimum wind chill of -19 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blockbuster winter storm that impacted millions across the lower 48, bringing a combination of ice; sleet; snow; rain; and strong winds, brought widespread heavy snowfall accumulations to all of eastern New York from January 25-26th, 2026. ||An arctic blast made for dangerously cold conditions across eastern New York from January 23-24, creating a requisite cold airmass that supported snow as the primary precipitation type with this storm. The connection of the northern and southern stream flow regimes then steered a potent, Gulf-originating low pressure system to the northeast. Strong to anomalous low- to mid-level dynamics drove impressive moisture transport from the Gulf and western Atlantic in and across the region the morning of the impending storm, Sunday January 25th. Snow began Sunday morning, initially with a large dendritic growth zone courtesy of persistent arctic air being filtered into the region from northerly flow about a nearby surface high to the northeast. However, as the storm tracked closer, its associated warm nose modified the thermal profile and shrunk and lifted the dendritic growth zone into the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere by the afternoon. Snowfall rates that initially looked to reach up to 2-4 per hour within laterally translating snow bands therefore became limited to 1-2 per hour courtesy of the disconnect between the best low-level forcing and the dendritic growth zone. Still, a widespread 10-20 of snow fell across the region between Sunday, January 25th and Monday, January 26th. ||The expansive impacts that this storm had on New York State as a whole led Governor Kathy Hochul to declare a widespread State of Emergency which encompassed all of our portion of eastern New York. NWS Albany received reports of numerous school and road closures in addition to traffic accidents across eastern New York. ||Key Impacts: state of emergency declarations, road closures, school closures, travel restrictions, traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 15.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blockbuster winter storm that impacted millions across the lower 48, bringing a combination of ice; sleet; snow; rain; and strong winds, brought widespread heavy snowfall accumulations to all of eastern New York from January 25-26th, 2026. ||An arctic blast made for dangerously cold conditions across eastern New York from January 23-24, creating a requisite cold airmass that supported snow as the primary precipitation type with this storm. The connection of the northern and southern stream flow regimes then steered a potent, Gulf-originating low pressure system to the northeast. Strong to anomalous low- to mid-level dynamics drove impressive moisture transport from the Gulf and western Atlantic in and across the region the morning of the impending storm, Sunday January 25th. Snow began Sunday morning, initially with a large dendritic growth zone courtesy of persistent arctic air being filtered into the region from northerly flow about a nearby surface high to the northeast. However, as the storm tracked closer, its associated warm nose modified the thermal profile and shrunk and lifted the dendritic growth zone into the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere by the afternoon. Snowfall rates that initially looked to reach up to 2-4 per hour within laterally translating snow bands therefore became limited to 1-2 per hour courtesy of the disconnect between the best low-level forcing and the dendritic growth zone. Still, a widespread 10-20 of snow fell across the region between Sunday, January 25th and Monday, January 26th. ||The expansive impacts that this storm had on New York State as a whole led Governor Kathy Hochul to declare a widespread State of Emergency which encompassed all of our portion of eastern New York. NWS Albany received reports of numerous school and road closures in addition to traffic accidents across eastern New York. ||Key Impacts: state of emergency declarations, road closures, school closures, travel restrictions, traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 12.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent polar airmass created a prolonged period of frigid conditions across eastern New York for the final days of January into the very beginning of February. Bitter cold air temperatures combined with periods of gusty winds and fresh, deep snowpack made for several days worth of dangerously cold conditions. ||The cold outbreak began early on January 29th where the most dangerously cold minimum wind chill values, or feels-like temperatures, were noted across the higher terrain areas of the Southern Adirondacks, Eastern Catskills, Mohawk Valley, and Taconics. However, by the following night, widespread dangerously cold temperatures overtook the region with minimum air temperatures ranging from -22 to 6 degrees Fahrenheit and minimum feels-like temperatures spanning -36 to -2 degrees Fahrenheit. A brief respite came on January 30th when extensive cloud cover maintained sufficient insulation to limit temperature falls, but by the next night, February 1, minimum feels-like temperatures once again fell to -34 to -5 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -3 degrees Fahrenheit recorded with a minimum wind chill of -18 degrees Fahrenheit.
